mission.title: Install and Configure Velero (Backup & Restore Operator)
mission.description: Velero is a backup and restore operator for Kubernetes that allows you to back up your cluster resources and persistent volumes, as well as migrate them between clusters. It is particularly useful for disaster recovery and data migration scenarios. Key trade-offs include the need for proper configuration of cloud storage backends and potential complexities in restoring large datasets.

steps.title: Add the Velero Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Velero Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add vmware-tanzu https://vmware-tanzu.github.io/helm-charts

```

> This is the official repository for Velero.

steps.title: Install Velero CRDs
steps.description:
This step installs the Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) required for Velero to function.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://github.com/vmware-tanzu/velero/releases/download/v1.18.0/velero-crds.yaml

```

> This installs the necessary CRDs for Velero.

steps.title: Install Velero using Helm
steps.description:
This step installs Velero in the specified namespace with the required configuration.

```bash

helm install velero vmware-tanzu/velero --namespace velero --create-namespace --version 1.18.0 --set configuration.provider=aws --set configuration.backupStorageLocation.name=aws --set configuration.backupStorageLocation.bucket=my-velero-bucket --set configuration.volumeSnapshotLocation.name=aws --set configuration.volumeSnapshotLocation.region=us-west-2

```

> Replace 'my-velero-bucket' with your actual S3 bucket name.
commands: helm install velero vmware-tanzu/velero --namespace velero --create-namespace --version 1.18.0 --set configuration.provider=aws --set configuration.backupStorageLocation.name=aws --set configuration.backupStorageLocation.bucket=my-velero-bucket --set configuration.volumeSnapshotLocation.name=aws --set configuration.volumeSnapshotLocation.region=us-west-2

steps.title: Create a Backup
steps.description:
This step creates a backup of your Kubernetes resources and persistent volumes.

```bash

velero backup create my-backup --include-namespaces my-namespace

```

> Replace 'my-namespace' with the namespace you want to back up.

steps.title: Verify Velero Installation
steps.description:
This step checks if Velero is installed and running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n velero

```

> You should see the Velero server pod running.

uninstall.title: Uninstall Velero
uninstall.description:
This step removes Velero and its associated resources. Be aware that this will delete all backups and data associated with Velero.

```bash

helm uninstall velero --namespace velero

kubectl delete namespace velero

```

> This will remove the Velero release.
> This will delete the Velero namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade Velero
upgrade.description:
This step includes backing up existing data, upgrading Velero, and verifying the upgrade.

```bash

velero backup create pre-upgrade-backup --include-namespaces my-namespace

helm upgrade velero vmware-tanzu/velero --namespace velero --version 1.18.0

kubectl get pods -n velero

```

> Create a backup before upgrading.
> Upgrade to the specified version.
> Verify that the Velero pods are running after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to create backup: unable to create backup storage location'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This error indicates that Velero cannot access the specified backup storage location.

**Fix:**
Check your S3 bucket permissions and ensure that the bucket exists.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'velero server is not running'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The Velero server pod may not be running due to configuration issues.

**Fix:**
Run 'kubectl logs deployment/velero -n velero' to check the logs for errors.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Backup failed: unable to find persistent volume'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the specified persistent volume is not available or has been deleted.

**Fix:**
Verify the existence of the persistent volume and ensure it is correctly configured.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to restore backup: resource already exists'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This error occurs when attempting to restore resources that already exist in the target namespace.

**Fix:**
Use the '--overwrite' flag when restoring to replace existing resources.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for Kubernetes 1.35, improved AWS S3 integration, and enhanced logging features.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated support for Kubernetes versions below 1.25.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from v1.17 or earlier, ensure that your backup storage location is correctly configured.

resolution.summary: You should see the Velero server pod running with a status of 'Running' when executing 'kubectl get pods -n velero'.
codeSnippets: helm install velero vmware-tanzu/velero --namespace velero --create-namespace --version 1.18.0 --set configuration.provider=aws --set configuration.backupStorageLocation.name=aws --set configuration.backupStorageLocation.bucket=my-velero-bucket --set configuration.volumeSnapshotLocation.name=aws --set configuration.volumeSnapshotLocation.region=us-west-2

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the required tools installed.
